glossary.definition: A slip of paper or electronic method used by voters to cast their vote in an election.
A person who is running for political office in an election.
A geographical area represented by an elected official, such as a Member of Parliament or Congress representative.
An alliance of political parties that come together to form a government or achieve a common goal.
A system of government where power rests with the people, who exercise that power through voting and participation.
In the USA, the group of 538 electors who formally cast votes to elect the President, rather than direct popular vote.
The method by which votes are cast, counted, and converted into seats or offices.
All the people in a country or area who are entitled to vote in an election.
An electoral system where the candidate with the most votes in a constituency wins, even without an absolute majority.
The right to vote in political elections, also known as suffrage.
An election held across the entire country to choose representatives for the whole legislature.
The process of creating a government after an election, typically involving the party with the most seats forming the executive.
The person currently holding a political office who is running for re-election.
More than half of the total number of votes or seats, often required to win or form a government.
A political party's published statement of their policies, principles, and plans for their time in office.
The formal process of officially selecting a candidate to run for a political office.
The legislative body of a country, consisting of elected representatives who make laws.
The location where voters go to cast their votes during an election.
An electoral system where the number of seats a party wins is proportional to the number of votes they receive.
The minimum number of members that must be present to conduct business in a legislative body.
A special election that allows voters to remove an elected official before their term ends.
A direct vote by the entire electorate on a specific issue or proposal, rather than electing representatives.
The act of one person or group acting or speaking on behalf of another in political matters.
A second round of voting between the top candidates when no candidate achieves a required majority in the first round.
The right to vote in elections, also known as the franchise.
The length of time an elected official serves in office before facing re-election.
The percentage or number of eligible voters who actually vote in an election.
A mechanical, electronic, or digital device used to tabulate votes cast by voters.
The process by which people officially enroll themselves to be eligible to vote in elections.
The geographical limits that define a constituency or electoral district.
